subject: ratios_proportions
explanation: Student leaves ratios in unsimplified form, e.g., writes 15 : 25 instead of 3 : 5.
why_students_think_this: Has not internalized that equivalent ratios exist, or doesn't recognize HCF application in ratios.
grade8_impact: Errors in rate, percentage, and proportion problems throughout Grade 8.
grade12_impact: Will struggle with trigonometric ratios and algebraic fractions in simplified form.
root_cause: Weak connection between fractions and ratios; HCF concept not applied to ratios

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Simplifies only one term of a ratio
explanation: Divides only the first or second term by the HCF, leaving the other unchanged.
why_students_think_this: Procedurally applies division to one number without understanding that both terms must be divided by the same value.
grade8_impact: Will produce incorrect simplified forms across all ratio and fraction topics.
grade12_impact: Algebraic fraction simplification errors will persist.
root_cause: Procedural gap: doesn't understand that simplification requires the same operation on both terms

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Reverses the order of ratio terms
explanation: Writes A : B as B : A, reversing the relationship between the two quantities.
why_students_think_this: Doesn't understand that the order of a ratio is meaningful and context-dependent.
grade8_impact: Will invert relationships in proportion problems, getting reciprocal answers.
grade12_impact: Errors in trigonometric ratios and rate comparisons.
root_cause: Insufficient understanding of the directed nature of ratio notation

subject: ratios_proportions
explanation: Uses addition/subtraction to scale quantities instead of multiplication/division. For example, if 4 items cost ₹36, adds ₹3 per item for next 3 items instead of finding unit rate.
why_students_think_this: Addition is the first operation students master; it feels intuitive to 'add the same amount'. Multiplicative scaling requires an abstraction many students haven't made.
grade8_impact: Fundamental barrier to ratio, proportion, percentage, and algebra.
grade12_impact: Will fail to understand exponential growth, compound interest, and rate of change.
root_cause: Has not made the transition from additive to multiplicative reasoning — a key developmental milestone

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Skips unit rate — multiplies without dividing first
explanation: When given a rate for multiple items, multiplies the new quantity by the total cost/distance instead of first finding the per-unit value.
why_students_think_this: Sees multiplication as the 'scaling operation' and skips the division step that normalizes to unit rate.
grade8_impact: Errors in speed-distance-time, pricing, and all rate problems.
grade12_impact: Cannot work with rates, gradients, or derivatives conceptually.
root_cause: Incomplete two-step proportional reasoning — can scale up but doesn't normalize first

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Treats ratio directly as a fraction without considering total parts
explanation: Interprets a ratio a : b directly as the fraction a/b, without recognizing that the fraction of the whole is a/(a+b).
why_students_think_this: Ratios and fractions look similar. Students transfer fraction notation directly without understanding the part-whole vs part-part distinction.
grade8_impact: Errors in profit-sharing, mixture problems, and all 'divide in ratio' contexts.
grade12_impact: Probabilistic reasoning and statistics will be affected.
root_cause: Conflation of part-part ratio with part-whole fraction

subject: ratios_proportions
explanation: Uses cross-multiplication as a rote procedure without knowing when it applies or what it means, leading to incorrect setups.
why_students_think_this: Cross-multiplication is taught as a trick for proportions. Students over-generalise and apply it to any two fractions or ratio expressions.
grade8_impact: Wrong answers on proportion problems where the ratio must be set up correctly before cross-multiplying.
grade12_impact: Algebraic manipulation errors when solving equations involving fractions.

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Inverts the proportion — applies direct proportion instead of inverse or vice versa
explanation: When quantities are inversely proportional, student treats them as directly proportional (or vice versa), leading to a reciprocal answer.
why_students_think_this: Direct proportion is encountered first and most often. Students default to it even when the context implies inverse proportion.
grade8_impact: Errors in work-time, speed-time, and other inverse proportion problems.
grade12_impact: Cannot distinguish between direct and inverse variation in physics or economics.
root_cause: Insufficient exposure to inverse proportion contexts; defaults to direct proportion

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Interprets ratio as a difference instead of a quotient
explanation: Uses subtraction to compare two quantities instead of division, treating 6 : 4 as 'two more' rather than '1.5 times as many'.
why_students_think_this: Subtraction is the default comparison operation for students. Understanding ratio as a multiplicative comparison requires explicit instruction.
grade8_impact: Cannot correctly interpret or set up any ratio or proportion problem.
grade12_impact: Weak foundation for rate of change, scale factors, and similarity in geometry.
root_cause: Has not built the concept of ratio as a multiplicative comparison

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Believes larger ratio numbers mean greater precision
explanation: Student thinks a ratio with larger numbers (e.g., 20 : 60) is more accurate or precise than its simplified form (1 : 3).
why_students_think_this: May be conflating with measurement precision where more decimal places = more accuracy.
grade8_impact: Conceptual confusion about ratio equivalence; will not simplify ratios voluntarily.
grade12_impact: Minor impact, but reflects weak number sense about equivalent forms.
root_cause: Overgeneralisation from measurement contexts to ratio contexts

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Believes both terms of a ratio must be greater than 1
explanation: Student incorrectly thinks ratios cannot contain 1 or that each term must be ≥ 2.
why_students_think_this: May have only seen ratios in the form 2 : 3 or 3 : 5 and overgeneralised the pattern.
grade8_impact: Minor — will occasionally reject correct simplified ratios.

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Divides a quantity equally instead of in proportion
explanation: When asked to divide a quantity in a given ratio, student simply divides by the number of people/parts equally.
why_students_think_this: Equal sharing is the default mental model for division. The idea that shares can be unequal but proportional requires additional reasoning.
grade8_impact: Errors in profit-sharing, recipe scaling, and all partition-by-ratio problems.
grade12_impact: Cannot apply proportional reasoning in economics, statistics, or real-world finance.
root_cause: Default mental model of division as equal sharing, not proportional sharing

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Computes incorrect unit rate through arithmetic error
explanation: Sets up the unit rate correctly but makes an arithmetic error in computing it (e.g., 36 ÷ 4 ≠ 8).
why_students_think_this: Understands the method but makes a calculation slip, often by halving instead of dividing by the correct divisor.
grade8_impact: Low severity — method is sound; arithmetic practice will resolve.
grade12_impact: Minimal long-term impact if underlying understanding is correct.
root_cause: Arithmetic fact gap, not conceptual error

subject: ratios_proportions
misconception_name: Finds total mixture instead of one part
explanation: When asked for one component of a mixture, student computes the total amount using all ratio parts instead of extracting the required component.
why_students_think_this: Confuses 'how much altogether' with 'how much of this component'. Doesn't distinguish between total and part in ratio problems.
grade8_impact: Errors in chemistry mixture, recipe, and alloy-type problems.
grade12_impact: Will affect concentration calculations and mixture problems in chemistry.
root_cause: Incomplete reading of the question; doesn't identify which part is being asked for
